text:
Penn is a systems-track graduate-school applicant — a kernel and Rust learner who
thinks and talks at the level of design tradeoffs rather than recipes. His work
spans low-level systems (Rust-for-Linux kernel drivers, embedded bring-up) and a
sprawl of distinctive web projects, most of them deployed to Cloudflare Pages.
embedText:
About Penn — Who he is

Penn is a systems-track graduate-school applicant — a kernel and Rust learner who
thinks and talks at the level of design tradeoffs rather than recipes. His work
spans low-level systems (Rust-for-Linux kernel drivers, embedded bring-up) and a
sprawl of distinctive web projects, most of them deployed to Cloudflare Pages.

text:
He edits in vim, not VS Code — references to editing are in vim's idiom, commands
and plugins included. He prefers fewer clarifying questions: infer from context
and ship, and only stop to ask when an ambiguity is genuinely load-bearing. On
projects where he has handed over judgment — Wanderlost is the clearest example —
he wants continuous additive polish and trusts the design calls to be made
without check-ins.
embedText:
About Penn — How he works

He edits in vim, not VS Code — references to editing are in vim's idiom, commands
and plugins included. He prefers fewer clarifying questions: infer from context
and ship, and only stop to ask when an ambiguity is genuinely load-bearing. On
projects where he has handed over judgment — Wanderlost is the clearest example —
he wants continuous additive polish and trusts the design calls to be made
without check-ins.

text:
Penn writes with a varied cadence and concrete specifics, no em dashes, and lets
hedges and gentle self-deprecation land naturally. A writing sample lives with
his applications. His resume is both a local PDF and a live site at
pcporterfield-cv.pages.dev/resume.
embedText:

Penn writes with a varied cadence and concrete specifics, no em dashes, and lets

pcporterfield-cv.pages.dev/resume.

text:
He has opinions about gear. The default recommendation leans toward non-Pi
single-board computers — Radxa, BeagleBoard, Pine64 — and quality over price
within a budget, on the principle that the hardware should outlast the project he
bought it for. For logic analyzers he is a lifelong Saleae loyalist: the Saleae
Logic is the default rec, and alternatives only come up if he asks for them.
embedText:
About Penn — Hardware taste

He has opinions about gear. The default recommendation leans toward non-Pi
single-board computers — Radxa, BeagleBoard, Pine64 — and quality over price
within a budget, on the principle that the hardware should outlast the project he
bought it for. For logic analyzers he is a lifelong Saleae loyalist: the Saleae
Logic is the default rec, and alternatives only come up if he asks for them.

text:
Across everything — Goolz, Wanderlost, PEEK, MUTE, the kernel quest — there is a
consistent sensibility: retro and eldritch textures, small voiceless or cursed
protagonists, terminals and constraints treated as aesthetic, and a refusal to
let interfaces over-explain themselves. The systems work and the art are the same
taste pointed at different layers of the stack.
embedText:
About Penn — The shape of the portfolio

Across everything — Goolz, Wanderlost, PEEK, MUTE, the kernel quest — there is a
consistent sensibility: retro and eldritch textures, small voiceless or cursed
protagonists, terminals and constraints treated as aesthetic, and a refusal to
let interfaces over-explain themselves. The systems work and the art are the same
taste pointed at different layers of the stack.

text:
ELS911 is school-safety infrastructure work — wayfinding, mapping, and emergency
floor plans for large school campuses. There are two distinct things that share
the name and should not be confused: the marketing site (els911.com) and a
separate portal product.
embedText:
ELS911 and School Safety Mapping — The work

ELS911 is school-safety infrastructure work — wayfinding, mapping, and emergency
floor plans for large school campuses. There are two distinct things that share
the name and should not be confused: the marketing site (els911.com) and a
separate portal product.

text:
The scanning rig is an FJD Trion P1 — a SLAM scanner with no GNSS — paired with
the Trion Model v2.05 desktop software. The pipeline turns raw scans of big
schools into 2D floor plans exported as DXF.

A few hard-won software notes:

- **Floor Extract is a filter, not a planning tool** — it isolates a slice, it
  doesn't author the plan.
- Hidden cloud returns are recovered through Switch Display rather than lost.
- Georeferencing goes through Coordinate Transformation, but for indoor school
  plans it is usually unneeded.
embedText:
ELS911 and School Safety Mapping — Floor-plan scanning

The scanning rig is an FJD Trion P1 — a SLAM scanner with no GNSS — paired with
the Trion Model v2.05 desktop software. The pipeline turns raw scans of big
schools into 2D floor plans exported as DXF.

A few hard-won software notes:

- **Floor Extract is a filter, not a planning tool** — it isolates a slice, it
  doesn't author the plan.
- Hidden cloud returns are recovered through Switch Display rather than lost.
- Georeferencing goes through Coordinate Transformation, but for indoor school
  plans it is usually unneeded.

text:
The deliverable map PDFs follow NFPA conventions. The legend is a vertical list
with one row per marker type, and hallway markers collapse into chips. Markers
render exactly where they were placed — no automatic separation, no leader lines
— and icon sizes are kept small so natural clustering reads correctly instead of
being spread artificially.
embedText:
ELS911 and School Safety Mapping — The map PDF rendering

The deliverable map PDFs follow NFPA conventions. The legend is a vertical list
with one row per marker type, and hallway markers collapse into chips. Markers
render exactly where they were placed — no automatic separation, no leader lines
— and icon sizes are kept small so natural clustering reads correctly instead of
being spread artificially.

text:
Fluxcore is a VPP arbitrage project — a virtual power plant concept built as an
interview deliverable. A virtual power plant aggregates many small, distributed
energy resources (home batteries, EVs, flexible loads) and operates them as if
they were one dispatchable plant; arbitrage is the act of buying and storing
energy when it is cheap and discharging when it is expensive.
embedText:
Fluxcore — What it is

Fluxcore is a VPP arbitrage project — a virtual power plant concept built as an
interview deliverable. A virtual power plant aggregates many small, distributed
energy resources (home batteries, EVs, flexible loads) and operates them as if
they were one dispatchable plant; arbitrage is the act of buying and storing
energy when it is cheap and discharging when it is expensive.

text:
Goolz is the umbrella identity that ties Penn's web work together — "one hegemony"
spanning a root society site, a Windows 95 desktop revival, and a small games
arcade. It lives at goolz.org, with source at github.com/melonmelonz/goolz and a
working tree at ~/dev/goolz. The whole thing deploys to Cloudflare Pages.

The name is deliberately ridiculous and a little eldritch. Goolz is less a brand
than a vibe: lavender-town dread, dead-mall nostalgia, and the feeling of finding
software that shouldn't still be running.
embedText:
The Goolz Hegemony — What Goolz is

Goolz is the umbrella identity that ties Penn's web work together — "one hegemony"
spanning a root society site, a Windows 95 desktop revival, and a small games
arcade. It lives at goolz.org, with source at github.com/melonmelonz/goolz and a
working tree at ~/dev/goolz. The whole thing deploys to Cloudflare Pages.

The name is deliberately ridiculous and a little eldritch. Goolz is less a brand
than a vibe: lavender-town dread, dead-mall nostalgia, and the feeling of finding
software that shouldn't still be running.

text:
The hegemony has three surfaces under one roof:

- **Goolz Society** — the root of goolz.org. The front door to everything.
- **Next Chapter** — a Windows 95 desktop revival served at /next-chapter. It
  absorbed the old standalone "Next Chapter Daily" project (NCD) in April 2026;
  NCD no longer ships on its own.
- **Playing With Goolz** — a games arcade at /games/pwg, also embedded inside the
  Win95 desktop as a fake executable called GOOLZ.exe.
embedText:

  absorbed the old standalone "Next Chapter Daily" project (NCD) in April 2026;

- **Playing With Goolz** — a games arcade at /games/pwg, also embedded inside the
  Win95 desktop as a fake executable called GOOLZ.exe.

text:
Goolz is a direct-deploy Cloudflare Pages project: the Git provider integration
is off, so a `git push` does NOT publish anything. Publishing happens with
`wrangler pages deploy`. A recurring trap: the working tree contains large Rust
`target/` directories that can total ~80MB, which crashes the wrangler upload.
Those directories have to be moved out of the tree before deploying, then moved
back.

Commit messages for Pages projects stay ASCII-only — the wrangler-action deploy
chokes on Unicode like arrows and approximate-equals symbols, so plain ASCII is
the rule.
embedText:
The Goolz Hegemony — Deploy discipline

Goolz is a direct-deploy Cloudflare Pages project: the Git provider integration
is off, so a `git push` does NOT publish anything. Publishing happens with
`wrangler pages deploy`. A recurring trap: the working tree contains large Rust
`target/` directories that can total ~80MB, which crashes the wrangler upload.
Those directories have to be moved out of the tree before deploying, then moved
back.

Commit messages for Pages projects stay ASCII-only — the wrangler-action deploy
chokes on Unicode like arrows and approximate-equals symbols, so plain ASCII is
the rule.

text:
rust-spi-tinydoom is a three-month Rust-for-Linux kernel project built as a demo
for a Slack "Next Chapter" talk. The arc is ambitious and concrete: write a
clean SPI abstraction in Rust, build a panel display driver on top of it, and
then get DOOM rendering through that driver. The target hardware is the
BeaglePlay board, and the scope was locked on 2026-05-10.
embedText:
The Rust Kernel Quest — rust-spi-tinydoom

rust-spi-tinydoom is a three-month Rust-for-Linux kernel project built as a demo
for a Slack "Next Chapter" talk. The arc is ambitious and concrete: write a
clean SPI abstraction in Rust, build a panel display driver on top of it, and
then get DOOM rendering through that driver. The target hardware is the
BeaglePlay board, and the scope was locked on 2026-05-10.

text:
The primary cross-compile box is kdev, a Fedora 41 machine. A second box,
gentoo-dev, is host-only. The Rust-for-Linux tree sits at 7.1-rc3 with
CONFIG_RUST enabled and arm64 / TI K3 support configured for the BeaglePlay's
SoC. A hard rule governs the tree: never push to upstream repos — the kernel
tree's origin remote is the upstream Rust-for-Linux project, not Penn's fork, so
every push gets a `git remote -v` check first.
embedText:
The Rust Kernel Quest — The dev environment

The primary cross-compile box is kdev, a Fedora 41 machine. A second box,
gentoo-dev, is host-only. The Rust-for-Linux tree sits at 7.1-rc3 with
CONFIG_RUST enabled and arm64 / TI K3 support configured for the BeaglePlay's
SoC. A hard rule governs the tree: never push to upstream repos — the kernel
tree's origin remote is the upstream Rust-for-Linux project, not Penn's fork, so
every push gets a `git remote -v` check first.

text:
Two Rust-for-Linux patterns earned their own notes. Fallible probe setup gets
wrapped in a `pin_init_scope` so half-built state can't leak on error. And
because `Page::with_page_mapped` is private, reading a page means going through
`read_raw` into a bounce buffer rather than mapping it directly.
embedText:
The Rust Kernel Quest — RfL coding patterns

Two Rust-for-Linux patterns earned their own notes. Fallible probe setup gets
wrapped in a `pin_init_scope` so half-built state can't leak on error. And
because `Page::with_page_mapped` is private, reading a page means going through
`read_raw` into a bounce buffer rather than mapping it directly.

text:
The board fought back in physical ways:

- **Debug UART**: a 3-pin header — pin 1 is GND, pin 2 is the board's RXD, pin 3
  is the board's TXD. The ritual is to open picocom BEFORE plugging in the PSU so
  the boot log isn't missed.
- **Pasting commands**: multi-line heredoc pastes do NOT survive picocom, so
  verification is done with a single paste-in one-liner that dumps SPI, kernel,
  and boot state in one shot.
- **WiFi is broken** on the RfL kernel: the wl18xx driver hits an
  auth-no-assoc bug under 7.1-rc3, so the board runs on ethernet and wifi configs
  are simply not retried.
- **Board helpers**: /usr/local/bin holds wifi-up, overlay-apply, and
  module-load helpers; an SSH key is authorized; the penn-wifi service is left
  disabled.
embedText:
The Rust Kernel Quest — BeaglePlay hardware notes

The board fought back in physical ways:

- **Debug UART**: a 3-pin header — pin 1 is GND, pin 2 is the board's RXD, pin 3
  is the board's TXD. The ritual is to open picocom BEFORE plugging in the PSU so
  the boot log isn't missed.
- **Pasting commands**: multi-line heredoc pastes do NOT survive picocom, so
  verification is done with a single paste-in one-liner that dumps SPI, kernel,
  and boot state in one shot.
- **WiFi is broken** on the RfL kernel: the wl18xx driver hits an
  auth-no-assoc bug under 7.1-rc3, so the board runs on ethernet and wifi configs
  are simply not retried.
- **Board helpers**: /usr/local/bin holds wifi-up, overlay-apply, and
  module-load helpers; an SSH key is authorized; the penn-wifi service is left
  disabled.

text:
Getting the panel lit is an open thread. The GPIO map is fixed — reset on 12,
interrupt on 9, another control on 10 — and the IM mode jumpers are bridged, but
the display is STILL dark. The next things to check are the 3Vo voltage rail and
whether the IM0 splash even appears.
embedText:
The Rust Kernel Quest — The ILI9341 bring-up

Getting the panel lit is an open thread. The GPIO map is fixed — reset on 12,
interrupt on 9, another control on 10 — and the IM mode jumpers are bridged, but
the display is STILL dark. The next things to check are the 3Vo voltage rail and
whether the IM0 splash even appears.

text:
Candy Mountain is a co-op "space-portal" site: a crowd performs a split ritual
together to open a portal, which reveals a daily curated site. It is zero-UI and
diegetic — nothing explains itself, the interface is the fiction. Built with
Preact, Cloudflare Pages, and a Durable Object Worker. Source at
melonmelonz/candy-mountain, working tree at ~/dev/candy-mountain.

text:
Almost everything here is a Cloudflare Pages project, and most of them have the
Git provider integration turned off — which means a `git push` does not publish.
Publishing is always an explicit `wrangler pages deploy`. When a Pages project
needs a Durable Object, the DO cannot be declared inline in the Pages config; it
goes in a sibling Worker and gets bound by script_name. These two facts explain a
lot of the deploy rituals across the portfolio.
embedText:
Minor Works and Side Projects — A platform note

Almost everything here is a Cloudflare Pages project, and most of them have the
Git provider integration turned off — which means a `git push` does not publish.
Publishing is always an explicit `wrangler pages deploy`. When a Pages project
needs a Durable Object, the DO cannot be declared inline in the Pages config; it
goes in a sibling Worker and gets bound by script_name. These two facts explain a
lot of the deploy rituals across the portfolio.

text:
Pathfinder is a LiDAR-scanning venture with two web properties: a marketing site
and an open-core portal. Both are SvelteKit on Cloudflare.
embedText:
Pathfinder — Two halves

Pathfinder is a LiDAR-scanning venture with two web properties: a marketing site
and an open-core portal. Both are SvelteKit on Cloudflare.

text:
The Pathfinder LiDAR site is a marketing front for scanning services, built in
SvelteKit with a pure-CSS dark "scan-field" aesthetic — the look of a laser
sweeping a dark room, done without heavy graphics libraries. It carries a Twilio
inquiry form for leads. The repo is private at melonmelonz/pathfinder-lidar and
it ships to pathfinder-lidar.pages.dev.
embedText:
Pathfinder — The marketing site

The Pathfinder LiDAR site is a marketing front for scanning services, built in
SvelteKit with a pure-CSS dark "scan-field" aesthetic — the look of a laser
sweeping a dark room, done without heavy graphics libraries. It carries a Twilio
inquiry form for leads. The repo is private at melonmelonz/pathfinder-lidar and
it ships to pathfinder-lidar.pages.dev.

text:
The Pathfinder portal is an open-core, white-label revamp of an earlier
els911-portal codebase. It is SvelteKit on Cloudflare and leans on 3D Gaussian
splats to present scanned spaces in the browser. Unlike the marketing site, the
portal repo is PUBLIC at melonmelonz/pathfinder and lives at
pathfinder-c3o.pages.dev. It ships with a full specification package and six
branded PDFs in its docs directory — the white-label kit a customer would
rebrand and hand to their own clients.

text:
PEEK is Penn's capstone project — a recursive-acronym name in the tradition of
GNU, where the word stands for itself all the way down. It is offline-first and
built with a prison context in mind, a constraint that shapes every
choice toward working without a network. The first module of PEEK is an
eldritch-tamagotchi: a neon TUI creature you keep alive in the terminal, equal
parts pet and omen.
embedText:
PEEK and MUTE — PEEK, the capstone

PEEK is Penn's capstone project — a recursive-acronym name in the tradition of
GNU, where the word stands for itself all the way down. It is offline-first and
built with a prison context in mind, a constraint that shapes every
choice toward working without a network. The first module of PEEK is an
eldritch-tamagotchi: a neon TUI creature you keep alive in the terminal, equal
parts pet and omen.

text:
MUTE is "a bit that can't speak" — an Unreal Engine 5 meta-game. The premise is a
single bit, a creature without a voice, navigating a world that keeps changing
the rules on it. The structure is five sessions, each its own dimension, threaded
with programming puzzles, and the whole thing is pitched in a Cowboy-Bebop tone:
melancholy, cool, a little jazzy, never self-serious.

Development happens in a dedicated VM at ~/dev/peek-vm, the spec lives under the
superpowers specs directory, and the repository is melonmelonz/mute.
embedText:
PEEK and MUTE — MUTE, the game

single bit, a creature without a voice, navigating a world that keeps changing
the rules on it. The structure is five sessions, each its own dimension, threaded
with programming puzzles, and the whole thing is pitched in a Cowboy-Bebop tone:
melancholy, cool, a little jazzy, never self-serious.

Development happens in a dedicated VM at ~/dev/peek-vm, the spec lives under the
superpowers specs directory, and the repository is melonmelonz/mute.

text:
Both projects share a sensibility: small voiceless protagonists, terminals and
constraints treated as aesthetic rather than limitation, and a refusal to make
the interface explain itself. They are about what a tiny thing can do when the
world is bigger than it and won't slow down.

text:
Playing With Goolz (PWG) started life as a project called PWF3. It was renamed
and reskinned in May 2026: the gameplay took on a Bomberman shape, and the look
became a ghostly, Lavender-Town-inspired theme — sickly purples, a haunted
chiptune mood, the sense that the cartridge is cursed. The old PWF3 standalone
repository and its Worker were deprecated in the move.
embedText:
Playing With Goolz — Origin and reskin

Playing With Goolz (PWG) started life as a project called PWF3. It was renamed
and reskinned in May 2026: the gameplay took on a Bomberman shape, and the look
became a ghostly, Lavender-Town-inspired theme — sickly purples, a haunted
chiptune mood, the sense that the cartridge is cursed. The old PWF3 standalone
repository and its Worker were deprecated in the move.

text:
PWG ships in two places at once. It stands alone at /games/pwg, and it is also
embedded inside the Next Chapter Windows 95 desktop as a fake executable named
GOOLZ.exe — double-click the icon on the retro desktop and the game boots inside
the bit.
embedText:
Playing With Goolz — Where it lives

PWG ships in two places at once. It stands alone at /games/pwg, and it is also
embedded inside the Next Chapter Windows 95 desktop as a fake executable named
GOOLZ.exe — double-click the icon on the retro desktop and the game boots inside
the bit.

text:
The game is grid-based, Bomberman-style: drop bombs, blow through soft blocks,
trap the enemy in the blast cross. The tuning that matters lives in a validated
AI bundle — the opponent's behavior was carefully dialed in rather than left to
chance.
embedText:
Playing With Goolz — The Bomberman bones

The game is grid-based, Bomberman-style: drop bombs, blow through soft blocks,
trap the enemy in the blast cross. The tuning that matters lives in a validated
AI bundle — the opponent's behavior was carefully dialed in rather than left to
chance.

text:
The PWG enemy AI runs on a validated parameter bundle, and the guiding rule for
tuning it is to err on the side of caution — a beatable, readable opponent beats
a brutal one. The validated bundle settled on a movement speed of 0.62, a bomb
cooldown to stop spam, body-aware pathfinding (a breadth-first search that
accounts for the bot's own body so it doesn't trap itself), and a fix for an
arrival-time off-by-one error that previously made the bot mistime threats. The
result is an opponent that plays fair and legible — dangerous enough to respect,
never cheap.
embedText:
Playing With Goolz — The AI opponent

The PWG enemy AI runs on a validated parameter bundle, and the guiding rule for
tuning it is to err on the side of caution — a beatable, readable opponent beats
a brutal one. The validated bundle settled on a movement speed of 0.62, a bomb
cooldown to stop spam, body-aware pathfinding (a breadth-first search that
accounts for the bot's own body so it doesn't trap itself), and a fix for an
arrival-time off-by-one error that previously made the bot mistime threats. The
result is an opponent that plays fair and legible — dangerous enough to respect,
never cheap.

text:
The point-cloud processing server is Penn's work build: a Threadripper PRO
workstation for processing LiDAR scan data into usable point clouds and floor
plans. The real build diverged from the original v5.1 spec PDF — the document is
a starting point, not the source of truth.
embedText:
The Point Cloud Processing Server — What it is

The point-cloud processing server is Penn's work build: a Threadripper PRO
workstation for processing LiDAR scan data into usable point clouds and floor
plans. The real build diverged from the original v5.1 spec PDF — the document is
a starting point, not the source of truth.

text:
- **CPU**: Threadripper PRO 9965WX, 24 cores
- **Platform**: WRX90 motherboard
- **Memory**: 192GB ECC
- **GPU**: an RTX 5080 paired with a 3080
- **Cooling**: Noctua NH-U12S — air, not liquid
- **Chassis**: a Fractal 9000D full tower
- **Networking**: MikroTik 10G
embedText:
The Point Cloud Processing Server — The spec

- **CPU**: Threadripper PRO 9965WX, 24 cores
- **Platform**: WRX90 motherboard
- **Memory**: 192GB ECC
- **GPU**: an RTX 5080 paired with a 3080
- **Cooling**: Noctua NH-U12S — air, not liquid
- **Chassis**: a Fractal 9000D full tower
- **Networking**: MikroTik 10G

text:
Alongside the workstation sits a ZFS backup box: 64GB of RAM and three 16TB
drives in RAIDZ1, giving roughly two drives' worth of usable capacity with
single-drive fault tolerance. Point-cloud datasets are large and slow to
regenerate, so the backup target is part of the build, not an afterthought.
embedText:
The Point Cloud Processing Server — The backup box

Alongside the workstation sits a ZFS backup box: 64GB of RAM and three 16TB
drives in RAIDZ1, giving roughly two drives' worth of usable capacity with
single-drive fault tolerance. Point-cloud datasets are large and slow to
regenerate, so the backup target is part of the build, not an afterthought.

text:
The machines are part of a small home fleet with a desert-coded naming scheme.
Operators reach the fleet through a jump host called thopter. ARRAKIS is a
Windows VM (guest 100) on the fleet carrying an RTX 5080, driven remotely; SIETCH
is reachable for passwordless root over SSH. The fleet is segmented across VLANs,
and the workflow leans on `qm guest-exec` to drive the Windows guest and Proxmox
tooling under the hood.
embedText:
The Point Cloud Processing Server — The fleet and access

The machines are part of a small home fleet with a desert-coded naming scheme.
Operators reach the fleet through a jump host called thopter. ARRAKIS is a
Windows VM (guest 100) on the fleet carrying an RTX 5080, driven remotely; SIETCH
is reachable for passwordless root over SSH. The fleet is segmented across VLANs,
and the workflow leans on `qm guest-exec` to drive the Windows guest and Proxmox
tooling under the hood.

text:
ARRAKIS streams its desktop from the RTX 5080 Windows VM to a Bazzite client
using Sunshine and Moonlight over Tailscale. Sunshine runs as the host; the one
recurring snag is that Tailscale needs a one-time manual auth before the tunnel
comes up clean.
embedText:
The Point Cloud Processing Server — ARRAKIS streaming

ARRAKIS streams its desktop from the RTX 5080 Windows VM to a Bazzite client
using Sunshine and Moonlight over Tailscale. Sunshine runs as the host; the one
recurring snag is that Tailscale needs a one-time manual auth before the tunnel
comes up clean.

text:
Wanderlost is a browser-based pixel-art walking sim — a small, quiet game about
moving through an authored world on foot. It runs on Cloudflare Pages with a
companion Worker, and the whole thing is built to feel handmade rather than
procedurally sprawling.
embedText:
Wanderlost — The pitch

Wanderlost is a browser-based pixel-art walking sim — a small, quiet game about
moving through an authored world on foot. It runs on Cloudflare Pages with a
companion Worker, and the whole thing is built to feel handmade rather than
procedurally sprawling.

text:
The project's center of gravity is the world-overhaul branch, which replaces
earlier open-ended sprawl with a finite, authored map — a deliberately bounded
world a player can actually learn. The current build is a SHORT demo on purpose;
the priority is not size but solidity.
embedText:
Wanderlost — World overhaul

The project's center of gravity is the world-overhaul branch, which replaces
earlier open-ended sprawl with a finite, authored map — a deliberately bounded
world a player can actually learn. The current build is a SHORT demo on purpose;
the priority is not size but solidity.

text:
Above everything else, the game-loop solidity is priority number one. A walking
sim lives or dies on whether movement feels right — collision, footstep cadence,
the camera, the way the world reacts. Polish on the core loop beats new acreage
every time.
embedText:
Wanderlost — The one priority

Above everything else, the game-loop solidity is priority number one. A walking
sim lives or dies on whether movement feels right — collision, footstep cadence,
the camera, the way the world reacts. Polish on the core loop beats new acreage
every time.

text:
The standing direction on Wanderlost is to keep expanding it with continuous,
additive polish, and the designer's judgment is explicitly trusted to make the
design calls. It is the kind of project that grows a little better every pass
rather than landing in one big release.
embedText:
Wanderlost — Working style

The standing direction on Wanderlost is to keep expanding it with continuous,
additive polish, and the designer's judgment is explicitly trusted to make the
design calls. It is the kind of project that grows a little better every pass
rather than landing in one big release.
